hook: Former coal yard turned ramshackle free-flamenco tavern
facts: Occupies the auxiliary buildings of the 16th-century Corral de Cabañas, a coal warehouse until 1974
Taken over in the mid-1970s by the theatre and flamenco troupe La Cuadra during the Transition
Stands on the block traditionally tied to Samuel Leví, treasurer to Peter I of Castile
Declared a cultural asset of interest for its role in reviving the San Bartolomé barrio
w: They shovelled coal here until 1974, then a 1970s theatre troupe moved into the old yard and the nightly flamenco never stopped. No stage, no cover charge, just a sangria, a guitar and whoever feels the duende that evening. The block traces back to Samuel Levi, treasurer to a Castilian king. Soot, smoke and song.

hook: Baroque charity hospital hung with Valdés Leal's rotting corpses
facts: Refounded by Miguel Mañara, a reformed libertine often cited as a model for Don Juan
Church holds Valdés Leal's death allegories 'In ictu oculi' and 'Finis gloriae mundi'
Also displays Golden Age works by Murillo and a Pedro Roldán altarpiece
Brotherhood originally buried the executed and the drowned pulled from the river
legend: They say the rake Mañara renounced worldly life after a vision of his own funeral procession
w: A reformed rake, sometimes named as a model for Don Juan, refounded this brotherhood to bury the executed and the river's drowned. Inside hang Valdes Leal's grim death allegories, a bishop's corpse crawling with vermin under the words 'in the blink of an eye.' They say he turned pious after glimpsing his own funeral. Vanity, off the slab.

hook: Hidden semicircle of arcades behind the Cathedral, Sunday coin market
facts: Reached through three narrow passageways, easy to walk past entirely
Curved colonnade wraps a surviving stretch of Almohad city wall from 1184
Hosts a collectors' market each Sunday since 1997: coins, stamps, postcards
Built on the site of the lost Colegio de San Miguel
w: Three slim passages off the cathedral crowds open onto a quiet half-moon of painted arcades most visitors never find. The curve hugs a chunk of Almohad wall raised in 1184, older than the Giralda. Come Sunday and tables fill with coins, stamps and postcards, the soft clink of handled metal. A secret in plain sight.

hook: Roman streets and mosaics under the giant timber Setas
facts: Sits five metres below street level, beneath the Metropol Parasol mushrooms
Excavations span roughly 5,000 square metres of Roman houses and streets
Remains run from a 1st-century salting factory to 6th-century homes plus an Almohad house
Found when digging a planned car park; the architect redesigned around them
w: Crews digging a car park hit Roman Hispalis instead, so the architect crowned the find with timber mushrooms and tucked the ruins underneath. Walkways thread streets, mosaics and a 1st-century fish-salting plant, with an Almohad house layered on top. A whole city block of buried centuries, five metres down. Parking can wait.

hook: Baroque retirement home for old priests with a sunken-fountain patio
facts: Founded 1675 by canon Justino de Neve as a shelter for elderly, poor priests
Patio's stepped fountain sits below ground, designed by Bernardo Simón de Pineda
Briefly a textile factory after 1840 before the brotherhood reclaimed it
Now the Velázquez Centre, home to his attributed 'Santa Rufina'
w: Built in 1675 to house Seville's worn-out priests, this Baroque refuge centres on a patio so strange the fountain steps down into the earth rather than up, a trick for low water pressure. Once degraded to a textile mill, it now guards a Santa Rufina credited to Velazquez. Note: closed for restoration until 2027.

hook: Mudéjar-Renaissance palace and the symbolic start of Seville's Via Crucis
facts: Begun 1483; completed by Fadrique Enríquez de Rivera after his 1519 Jerusalem pilgrimage
Permanent residence of the Dukes of Medinaceli
Named for a tradition equating the route to Calvary's distance to Golgotha
Mixes Italian Renaissance, Mudéjar tilework and a courtyard of antique statues
legend: Tradition holds the processional distance to the Cruz del Campo matched Pilate's walk to Golgotha
w: A marquis came home from Jerusalem in 1519 and launched Seville's Stations of the Cross from his door, the walk to the Cruz del Campo supposedly matching Pilate's road to Golgotha, hence the name. Inside: Mudejar tiles, Roman busts and a courtyard fountain. Still the Medinaceli dukes' house. Pilgrimage by palace.

n: Iglesia de San Luis de los Franceses
hook: Dizzying Baroque rotunda of a former Jesuit novitiate
facts: Built 1699-1730, the masterpiece of architect Leonardo de Figueroa
Greek-cross plan under a cupola ringed by Solomonic columns
Frescoes by Lucas Valdés; altarpieces by Pedro Duque Cornejo
Deconsecrated after the Jesuits' expulsion; church, chapel and crypt now open
w: Leonardo de Figueroa spent three decades to 1730 raising this Jesuit novitiate chapel, then poured every Baroque trick into one giddy rotunda: twisting Solomonic columns, a frescoed dome, gold dripping from the altarpieces. The order got expelled and the church went quiet for years. Now the crypt's open too. Vertigo, gilded.

hook: Victorian shot tower now a working camera obscura over the city
facts: Built around 1890 as part of the San Francisco de Paula lead-shot factory
Molten lead was dropped down the 45-metre shaft to form pellets
Worked until 1950; restored for Expo 92
Camera obscura opened 2007, projecting a live panorama of Seville
w: Molten lead once fell the full 45 metres inside this brick stack, cooling into round shot before it hit the bottom, a factory that armed hunters until 1950. Now a lens and mirror up top throw a live, gliding panorama of Seville onto a dished table in the dark. Watch the city move without leaving the room. Rain made of metal.

hook: Toy hexagonal castle, Seville's first neo-Mudéjar building
facts: Built 1893 in striped two-tone brick by architect Juan Talavera y de la Vega
Seville's oldest example of the neo-Mudéjar style
Originally a guard house in the gardens of the Palacio de San Telmo
Now houses a tourist information office
legend: Popular tradition claims Princess Mercedes sat sewing here, though she died years before it was built
w: A striped little brick castle with turrets at every corner, raised in 1893 as a garden guard house and counted as Seville's first neo-Mudejar fancy. Legend nicknames it the Queen's Sewing Box, claiming Princess Mercedes stitched here, though she had died years before the first stone went down. A storybook on a park corner.

n: Columnas Romanas de la Calle Mármoles
hook: Three giant Roman columns marooned below the modern street
facts: Granite shafts about 8 metres tall, dated to the 2nd century AD under Hadrian
Likely from the portico of a Roman temple, perhaps to Hercules or Mars
Stand at their original level, roughly 4.5 metres below today's pavement
Two sister columns were moved to the Alameda de Hércules in 1578
legend: Tradition tied them to columns Hercules raised to mark where Seville would rise
w: Three granite columns stand sunk in a narrow lane, their feet 4.5 metres below the modern street because the city simply rose around them. Raised under Hadrian for a temple, maybe to Hercules. Legend credited the demigod himself with planting them to mark where Seville would one day rise. Their sisters went to the Alameda in 1578.

hook: Almohad public bathhouse buried in a quiet backstreet
facts: Almohad baths from the late 12th century, among the largest in al-Andalus
Patio ringed by columns with muqarnas capitals and star-shaped skylights
On Calle Baños, adjoining a Vera Cruz brotherhood chapel that co-owns them
Declared a cultural heritage site in 1996
legend: Name 'Moorish Queen's Baths' is folk tradition; far from the Alcazar, royalty likely never bathed here
w: Behind a plain San Vicente facade survive Almohad baths from the 1100s, once among the biggest in al-Andalus, their vaults pierced with star-shaped skylights. The romantic name credits a Moorish queen, but they sit too far from the Alcazar for royal soaks, so the title stays pure legend. Steam, marble and a borrowed crown.

hook: Inquisition headquarters excavated under Triana's market
facts: Almohad-origin fortress that housed the Spanish Inquisition from 1481 to 1785
Demolished in the 19th century and built over by the Triana food market
Foundations rediscovered in 1990 and opened as a museum of tolerance
Underground route passes reconstructed cells and the old dockside streets
w: For three centuries the Inquisition judged, jailed and condemned from this riverside castle, until they tore it down and laid a food market on top. The foundations resurfaced in 1990, so now you descend past tomato stalls into excavated cells and dock-bound streets. A museum of intolerance under the haggling. Heresy and ham.
